questions.text: Answer Part (a) [or]{.underline} Part (b). Do not attempt both parts. (a) "The limits of Union competence are governed by the principle of conferral. The use of the Union competences is governed by the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality" (Article 5 TEU (formerly Article 5 EC)). Explain the principle of conferral. Assess, by reference in particular to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, whether the principles of subsidiary and proportionality have, since their formal introduction by the Treaty of Maastricht in 1993, acted as an effective constraint on the use by the Union legislature of its law-making competence under the TFEU. Evaluate what impact the changes introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon, in particular by the 'Protocol on the Application of the Principles of Subsidiarity and Proportionality', have had, if any, in this regard. [Or]{.underline} (b) Article 15 TFEU (formerly Article 255(1) EC), which was originally inserted by the Treaty of Amsterdam with effect from 1st May 1999, provides that: "[A]ny citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, shall have a right of access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents, subject to the principles and the conditions to be defined in accordance with this paragraph". Analyse the extent to which this Treaty provision, EU legislation and the case-law of the Union judicature guarantees for Union citizens an effective right of public access to documents held by the institutions of the Union?

Answer part (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do not attempt both parts. (a) Describe briefly the most significant changes to the competences of the European Community and the powers of its institutions that were introduced by the Treaty on European Union (Maastricht Treaty) of 1991. Describe briefly the principal further significant changes that were introduced by the Treaty of Amsterdam of 1997. What, in your opinion, were the most significant changes to the legal order and competences of the European Union introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon of 2007? [Or]{.underline} (b) Having regard in particular to the case-law of the High and Supreme Courts interpreting the notion of "necessitated by obligations of membership" of what is now the European Union (and the European Atomic Energy Community) under Article 28.4.6 of the Constitution of Ireland, analyse critically the constitutional limits to transposing and implementing Union legislation, in particular directives, into Irish law by statutory instrument pursuant to the European Communities Act 1972 to 2009.

questions.description: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Court of Justice of the notions of a "court or…
questions.text: Discuss by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Court of Justice of the notions of a "court or tribunal" and of "a court or tribunal against whose decisions there is no judicial remedy in national law" for the purposes of Article 267 TFEU. Your answer should also 'explain what bodies in Ireland, apart from the courts established under the Constitution and by law, could fall within the scope of this first notion, and what courts, apart from the Supreme Court, could fall within the second notion. In what circumstances may courts "against whose decisions there are no judicial remedy in national law" decline, in conformity with the third subparagraph of Article 267 TFEU, to make a preliminary reference when a matter of EU law arises before them?

questions.description: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for…
questions.text: Write a note on any three of the following cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice: (i) Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR 1; (ii) Case 22/70 Commission v. Council ("ERTA")[1971] ECR 263; (iii) Case C-91/92 Faccini Dori [1994] ECR 1337; (iv) Cases C-46/93 and C-48/93 Brasserie du Pecheur v. Germany & Factortame III [1996] ECR 1-1029; (v) Case C-112/00 Schmidbereger v. Austria [2003] ECR I-5669 ; (vi) Case C-167/02 P Rothley & Ors v. European Parliament [2004] ECR I-3149; (vii) Case C-341/05 Laval un Partneri [2007] ECR I-11767; (viii) Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v. Council [2008] ECR I-6351; (ix) C-236/09 Association belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats & Others V. Conseil des ministres [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 1 March 2011).

questions.description: On 1 February 2008, West County Council ("WCC"), a local authority in the… Damages, Directive.
questions.text: On 1 February 2008, West County Council ("WCC"), a local authority in the (fictitious) EU Member State of Boomtonia, granted Windy Ltd 'special planning permission' ('SPP') for construction of a windfarm in West County. West County Council did not carry out an 'environmental detriment assessment' ('EDA') in respect of the application, as none was required under the planning law of the Boomtonia. On 1 July 2008, the ECJ decided in Commission v Boomtonia that the Boomtonian planning practice of granting SPP's without requiring EDA's to be carried out was incompatible with the (fictitious) EU EDA Directive. The Green Alliance, a group of local residents opposed to the development, wish to have the national court quash WCC's grant of SPP. In addition, Mr Farmer would like compensation for the reduction in the value of his property that has been occasioned by the grant of the SPP for the construction of the windfarm on lands adjacent to Mr. Farmer's farm. Advise the Green Alliance as to whether and, if so, in what circumstances, it may: (i) rely on the EDA Directive against WCC in order to have the grant of SPP quashed; (ii) rely on the EDA Directive against Windy Ltd to stop the development. Advise Mr Farmer as to whether he is: (iii) entitled to damages from Boomtonia for its breach of EU law; (iv) entitled to damages from Windy Ltd under EU law

Answer parts (i), (ii) [and]{.underline} (iii). (i) John, an Irish citizen, has been accepted onto a course of study at a Belgian university. The Belgian authorities refuse his application for the student maintenance grant provided for Belgian students, on the basis that he has not been resident in Belgium for 5 years before commencing his course of study. Belgium argues that its refusal is permitted under Article 24 of Directive 2005/38/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. John seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is lawful under EU law. [And]{.underline} (ii) Mairead is an Irish national, who lived and worked in the United Kingdom for several years before returning to Ireland last year, where after she met and married Hanam, a failed asylum seeker from Afghanistan. The Minister for Justice has recently served a notice of intention to deport on Hanam. Mairead has asked for advice as to whether Hanam has an EU-law based right to reside in Ireland as her spouse. Ireland argues that Directive 2004/38/EC does not apply as Mairead is an Irish citizen resident in Ireland. [And]{.underline} (iii) Heinz is an Austrian national who has recently moved to Ireland in search of employment. He applies for a job as a senior IT manager in the Department of Foreign Affairs, but is refused an interview on the basis that the post is reserved for Irish nationals. Ireland argues that EU law permits it to reserve some public service posts to nationals. Advise Heinz on whether this is so, and, if so, whether the exemption may be applied to the post in question. Set out your advice to John, Mairead and Heinz.

questions.description: Compare and contrast (i) the scope: (ii) definition of discrimination; and… Directive.
questions.text: Compare and contrast (i) the scope: (ii) definition of discrimination; and (iii) the availability of grounds of possible justification, as regards the prohibitions in EU law of discrimination on the grounds of: (i) sex; and (ii) age; and (iii) racial or ethnic origin. In your answer, refer to relevant Treaty provisions, directives and case-law of the Union judicature.

Answer Part (a), (b) [or]{.underline} (c). Do not attempt more than one part. (a) Assess critically the operation of the new decentralised system for the enforcement of EC competition law under Council Regulation (EC) No. 1/2003 of 16 December 2002 on the implementation of the rules of competition laid down in Articles 101 and 102 TFEU, which has been in force since 1 May 2004. Or (b) Discuss the approach of (i) the European Commission and (ii) the Union judicature, when interpreting and applying Article 101 TFEU, to the analysis of provisions restrictive or allegedly restrictive of competition in agreements between undertaking operating at different levels in a market, i.e. to what are known as vertical restraints. Has a 'rule of reason' been applied and are considerations that are unrelated to competition taken into account? [Or]{.underline} (c) "[...] Price competition is the essence of free and open competition which is the objective of Community policy to establish on the internal market. It favours more efficient firms and it is for the benefit of consumers both in the short and long run. Dominant firms not only have the right but should be encouraged to compete on price" (Opinion of Advocate General Fennelly in Case C-395/96 P Compagnie Maritime Belge v. Commission [2000] ECR I-1365). Discuss this view with particular reference to the case-law of the Union judicature relating to the constraints placed by Article 102 TFEU on the pricing activities, such as regards predatory pricing, price squeezing and excessive prices, of dominant firms.

Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) Having regards to both its role and its composition, analyse critically whether the Commission effectively "promote[s] the general interest of the Union" as required by Article 17(1) TEU. Your answer should particularly focus on the Commission's power of initiation in the legislative process, its function in ensuring the application generally of measures adopted by the Union institutions and its role as 'guardian of the Treaties', as well as referring, where relevant, to case-law of the Union judicature. [Or]{.underline} (B) With reference to relevant case-law, discuss critically the Court of Justice's approach to challenges to the legal basis of Union legislation, and in particular the principle that the choice of the legal basis for a measure "may not depend simply on an institution's conviction as to the objective pursued but must be based on objective factors which are amenable to judicial review" such as "the aim and the content of the measure" (see, e.g., Case C-300/89 Commission v Council ("Titanium Dioxide") [1991] ECRI-2867, paragraph 10).

questions.description: With reference to the Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Union judicature, explain the sources, legal…
questions.text: With reference to the Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Union judicature, explain the sources, legal status and scope of application of the general principles of Union law. Consider in particular the case-law on non-discrimination, fundamental rights and legitimate expectations.

Answer Part (A) [and]{.underline} Part (B). (A) Analyse by reference to relevant case-law of the Court of Justice the procedural safeguards afforded to Member States during the pre-litigation phase of infringement actions under Articles 258 and 259 TFEU, as well as those applicable to applications made by the Commission under Article 260(2) and 260(3) TFEU. [And]{.underline} (B) Assess the view that Articles 258 to 260 TFEU, as interpreted by the Court of Justice, provide an increasingly effective instrument for securing compliance by Member States with their Union law obligations. QUESTION Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following eight judgments, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how these issues where addressed by the Court of Justice: (i) Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR 1; (ii) Case C-376/98 Germany v. European Parliament and Council (Tobacco Advertising Directive) [2000] ECR I-8419; (iii) Case C-224/01 Kobler [2003] ECR I-10239 (iv) Case C-341/05 Laval un Partneri [2007] ECR I-11767; (v) Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v. Council [2008] ECR I-6351; (vi) C-236/09 Association belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats and Others v. Council [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 1 March 2011); (vii) Case C-34/09 Zambrano [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 8 March 2011); (viii) Joined Cases C-106/09 P and C-107/09 P Commission v. Government of Gibraltor and United Kingdom [2011] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 15 November 2011).

questions.description: Tomina is a surgeon of Italian nationality now working and resident in Rome who did a one-year post-graduate internship…
questions.text: Tomina is a surgeon of Italian nationality now working and resident in Rome who did a one-year post-graduate internship in surgery in an Irish hospital in 2003 working on average 60 hours per week during which she received no remuneration. She has just discovered that under a hypothetical 1992 directive on the acquisition of the title of medical specialist she should have been paid a "reasonable remuneration" and should not have been required to work on average more than a 48-hour week. The Directive should have been transposed into Irish law by no later than 31 December 1999. It has, however, not yet been implemented into Irish law. Tomina has sought your advice as whether she could bring an action in Ireland, relying upon the relevant provisions of the directive, against either the hospital concerned and/or Ireland for unpaid wages and/or damages for being forced to work an average 60-hour week, notwithstanding the provisions of the Statute of Limitations prescribing a general six- year limitation period in Irish Law. Does the fact that Italian law would provide a ten-year limitation period make any difference?

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of [any three]{.underline} of the following four (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of [any three]{.underline} of the following four (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Union law, particularly free movement law: (i) A statutory prohibition on the towing of trailers by mopeds and motorcycles, unless designed specifically for mopeds and motorcycles. No trailers of any sort are manufactured in Ireland. The Government argues the measure does not hinder the free movement of trailers as they may be used for other purposes and that the prohibition is necessary to ensure road safety. It however means that trailers designed specifically for mopeds and motorcycles require modification before being capable of being towed by other vehicles; (ii) A decision of the Minister for Justice and Equality to refuse an application for a permanent right of residence in Ireland by Tomasz, a Polish national who came to Ireland in 2006 and worked in Dublin for three years before being made redundant in 2009, but who has since been on jobseeker's allowance; (iii) A copy Amendment Act that prohibits the use of satellite decoders purchased or acquired outside of the state by , or on behalf of, undertakings active in the hospitality industry, including public houses, hotels and restaurants, in order to screen showings of live sporting events taking place outside the State; (iv) A Finance Act provision that allows natural persons resident in Ireland and who invest in companies established in Ireland to claim for tax purposes an exemption from income tax on dividends distributed by such companies in the form of shares in their subsidiaries up to a maximum share value of €5,000 in any given tax year.

Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) As a specialists in Union anti-discrimination law, you have been asked to advise regarding the following two client's situations: <!-- --> (i) Joan has worked as a manager for a health authority for the past five years. Previously, following her graduation from university, she worked in the private sector for several years before becoming a homemaker for a period of six years. She has discovered that a number of male colleagues, who hold similar managerial positions to Joan for between two and four years, are all paid more than her. In response to Joan's complaint, the health authority argues that the differences in pay are justified by the male comparator's longer service records with the authority, albeit mostly at lower grades than Joan. Advise Joan as to the lawfulness of the authority's use of the criterion of length of service as a determinant of pay under Union sex discrimination law; (ii) Mary is a waitress who has undergone in vitro fertilisation treatment. As a result of an incident which occurred during this treatment she became unwell and was certified as unfit for work by her general practitioner for a week. Upon her return to work, she was dismissed by her employer. However, by the time of that return to work in vitro fertilisation of her ova with her partner's sperm cells had already taken place, although the fertilised ova were not transferred into Mary's uterus until several days later Advise Mary as to whether she would have a right under Union equal treatment directives to challenge the fairness of her dismissal. [Or]{.underline} (B) Analyse by reference to relevant case-law how the expression "the place where the harmful event occurred or may occur" in Article 5 (3) of the 'Brussels Regulation' (Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters) has been interpreted by the Court of Justice.

questions.description: Big Ship plc is a maritime undertaking that operates various passenger and freight services between Ireland and the…
questions.text: Big Ship plc is a maritime undertaking that operates various passenger and freight services between Ireland and the United Kingdom and between the United Kingdom and a number of other Union Member States. It also owns several of the ports it uses, including the post of Holyhead in Wales. Having invested heavily in a new computerised reservation system for its passenger services, Big Ship has decided progressively to change its sailing timetables on the route from the port of Dublin to the port of Holyhead to facilitate the sale of 'landlink' tickets to Irish-resident customers desirous of travelling by car to the European mainland via Great Britain, on the one hand , and to customers resident in mainland Europe wishing to travel by car to Ireland via Great Britain on the other. Solicitors acting on behalf of Long Beag Teo., a privately owned Irish ferry-operator that competes with the Big Ship on the Dublin to Holyhead route and purchases mooring and docking services from Big Ship at the port of Holyhead, have written to the latter's Irish solicitors complaining that the new timetable operated by Big Ship for its own services to and from the port of Holyhead makes it difficult , particularly at times of higher tide, for Long Beag to load and unload, there certain of its mixed passenger and freight vessels. Long Beag is threatening to complain to the Commission that Big Ship is breaching Union competition law and /or to bring legal proceedings in Ireland against Big Ship alleging such breaches. Long Beag has also recently launched new low-cost, freight-only service from Dublin port to Rotterdam in the Netherlands. Big Ship wishes to increase the frequency of its existing freight services from Dublin to Holyhead and from Harwich to Rotterdam to avoid losing market share to the new Long Beag service. However, it is fearful that this could breach Union completion rules as, in addition to owning Holyhead port, it holds approximately 60% of the market on the Dublin to Holyhead rout (but less than 50% of the market for freight traffic) and 40% overall if the market is defined as routes from Ireland to Great Britain. Moreover, it holds approximately a 30% market share on the Harwich to Rotterdam freight route, where it competes with several other carriers. No other carriers are currently offering a direct freight service from Dublin to Rotterdam. As an expert in Union competition law, Big Ship's Irish solicitors have asked you to draft an opinion for their client setting out for Big Ship, firstly, the types of action and/or complaint based on Union competition law that Long Beag could take against it , either in the Irish courts and / or with the Commission and, secondly, the likelihood of Long Beag's succeeding in any legal action taken and/or of the Commission's acting upon any complaint made and the likely nature of such possible action by the Commission. Your opinion should analyse the relevant markets and potential breaches of competition law that could be involved if Big Ship: (a) proceeds to introduce its proposed new timetable on the Dublin to Holyhead route; (b) increases the frequency of its existing freight services from Dublin to Holyhead and from Harwich to Rotterdam. Your opinion should also suggest any relevant less risky (from a Union competition law perspective) alternative business strategies that Big Ship might wish to consider adopting.
